author: @jasonhuxley674
text: I've been following this channel with interest as it is just plain fun but also, as I plan to move onto my sailboat and want alternatives to my existing solar solution (48v) be less dependent on the weather ;) The recent videos of harvesting from water have been great food for thought. It feels like there is mileage in a hybrid wave / air. Imagine a number of tubes with one end immersed in the water, two non-return valves near the top: 1 allowing external air into the tube from the atmosphere, the other allowing egress of that air into a connected manifold. Configure the only exit from the manifold to pass through a turbine and harvest energy from that.

author: @ruthe6017
text:
Dear Robert, 

Please could you help with re-designing freezer drawers.  The ones that came with the freezer cracked, replacements are £60+ and it's daft to replace the brittle plastic that cracked with the same brittle plastic!  I was thinking about different materials and thought about carbon fibre +/- silicone but would really love to get your take on it while it keeps whirling around my head when I should be doing other stuff.  It did occur to me that the wind turbine etc would also need to be suitable for below freezing so you and the rest of this tribe might have some suggestions?

Big fan, love the channel!

Ruth

author: @kadmow
text:
A pull cord will be the most impressive simple add on (easier to make than a turbine).

NB - general info: With respect to  a single rotating, body the torque doesn't change - the force decreases the longer the radius arm - Torque remains - and power is constant, to truly convert torque to speed (ie. Linear Power = Force * velocity,  Rotational Power = Torque * angular velocity)  a transmission - of some sort (gear, belt, string, chain, magnetic, etc...) is required. With a transmission, neglecting losses, Torque reduces while speed increases - Power is constant - vice versa.

author: @shanealexander9952
text:
In the US, there is an item used to turn the older fire alarm bells called a water moter gong.  It is a pre-made water to torque converter which turns the bell.  They use bushings instead of bearings but the setup is almost exactly like the air turbine you just made.  Also the blades are cheep plastic but it works wonderfully.  The indoor turbine assembly turns a steel bar through a tube in wall to the bell and striker.  Funny thing is I put a used one in my truck a week ago and examine it now and then.  The whole assembly is so close but just off.

Never thought of putting air through it but I'm sure it would work.  For now it's just turning the wheels in my mind.  The bushings can easily be bearings.  Anyway the frame is aluminum with a tin cover plate.

author: @kurdaitcha3509
text:
Generating electricity is BLOODY easy....
Humanity has so many ways to do that part its crazy!
The part we struggle with is storing it until we need to actually use it!

We do like to store it as electrons in batteries or capacitors since in that form its "ready to use".
Thats the ONLY 2 ways we have to store it like that - some kind of battery, or capacitor.
If you want to solve the energy crisis, dont focus on generation focus on storage!
"Build a better battery".....this generations equivalent of the old saying "build a better mousetrap"!

Seems easy....harder than it seems!

BTW....this is LITERALLY a scale model of a windfarm.
And even at this scale it includes the ONE part that makes it all work as intended....
A bloody battery!! A method of storing and building that charge until you want it.

Relying on "instant generation, immediate use" is something even schoolkids recognise as ridiculous!
Yet thats exactly what our power grids do!
(Our entire system here only has ONE. Refered to as "The Musk Battery" here in S.A.)

Over a 24 hour period I generate more energy than I consume, yet it is a slow trickle that needs to build up.
Not enough to use directly from the source, but AMPLE overall.
If you can find a better way to store electrons, you just found the holy grail !

There ARE other methods, but they ALL require conversion. They store energy in ways that need conversion back into electrical energy before it can be used in the final device.
Crack that nut, you just saved the world!
Good luck!

We have enough, why not use it.

Greetings from northern Finland!

I have had 5 of these light and a string of 8 lights on my window for couple of winters now. All solar powered. 

Removed the small 1,5V NiMh-batteries, connected the lights all together and to my 1,5V battery pack made of 8 NiMh-batteries from broken chargeable vacuum.

Only in mid winter the lights started to go out before the morning. Otherwise a pretty realible night/emergency light.

author: @StoryUponStory
text:
Heyhey, with regards to utilizing atmospheric electricity:

(TL;DR at the end of the comment)

I have been wondering if it could be possible to connect an atmospheric motor (akin to the corona motor you showed in your previous videos, appropriatly designed for the following purpose) to a fan, by that cause a surface (with heatsink) to be cooled to such a degree that the surface would collect dew from the aerial humidity, which then due to gravity would allow this water to be collected, thereby causing a continual supply of fresh drinkable water, for as long as the connection to the ions in the sky and grounding is stabilized. 

If the water would be collected higher in the sky, and enough of it, the force resulting from gravity could probably allow the flow of water to be connected to a water turbine also, per a "storage of X liters till flow into turbine is profitable" system.

In theory this should work wherever humidity is present, depending on environment.

With the switch patent you also had mentioned before, connecting the rod (the wire) in such a manner that the electricity flows through a coil probably in theory causes inductive heating (which usually is dependent on amps and AC while with this scenario it should probably still work with DC) due to jumping voltages resulting from the switch being triggered.

If true, dew collection would then be possible in "every" temperature.

TL;DR: Continual running of fan + inductive heating coil (caused by switch) due to electricity syphoned from the atmosphere = dew point collection "everywhere" and thereby drinkable water "everywhere", and "always".

I've also been wondering if just putting a wire into the earth deep enough would have the same electrostatic effect that corona motors utilize? Its still delta V.

I would love to hear your thoughts on this, possibly even a video of you delving into this concept.

Thanks. 🦔

replies.author: @unicornadrian1358
replies.text: You could build one to test your theory. Another way to help moisture drop out of the atmosphere is by lowering the air pressure. You could combine a fan with your heat sink on the upper side of an airfoil and see if that increase water yield.
